verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: “About the same time, a cow which a demon harassed met Martin as he was returning from Treves.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: That cow, leaving its proper herd, was accustomed to attack human beings, and had already seriously gored many with its horns.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: Now, when she was coming near us, those who followed her from a distance began to warn us, with a loud voice, to beware of her.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: But after she had in great fury come pretty near to us, with rage in her eyes, Martin, lifting up his hand, ordered the animal to halt, and she immediately stood stock-still at his word.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: Upon this, Martin perceived a demon sitting upon her back, and reproving it, he exclaimed, ‘Begone, thou deadly being; leave the innocent beast, and cease any longer to torment it.’

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: The evil spirit obeyed and departed.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: And the heifer had sense enough to understand that she was set free; for, peace being restored to her, she fell at the feet of the holy man; and on Martin directing her, she made for her own herd, and, quieter than any sheep, she joined the rest of the band.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: This also was the time at which he had no sensation of being burnt, although placed in the midst of the flames; but I do not think it necessary for me to give an account of this, because Sulpitius there, though passing over it in his book, has nevertheless pretty fully narrated it in the epistle which he sent to Eusebius, who was then a presbyter, and is now a bishop.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: I believe, Postumianus, you have either read this letter, or, if it is still unknown to you, you may easily obtain it, when you please, from the bookcase.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: I shall simply narrate particulars which he has omitted.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: “Well, on a certain occasion, when he was going round the various parishes, we came upon a band of huntsmen.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: The dogs were pursuing a hare, and the little animal was already much exhausted by the long run it had had.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: When it perceived no means of escape in the plains spreading far on every side, and was several times just on the point of being captured, it tried to delay the threatened death by frequent doublings.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: Now the blessed man pitied the danger of the creature with pious feelings, and commanded the dogs to give up following it, and to permit it to get safe away.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: Instantly, at the first command they heard, they stood quite still: one might have thought them bound, or rather arrested, so as to stand immovable in their own footprints.

verses.book: Dialogue II. Concerning the Virtues of St. Martin.
verses.text: In this way, through her pursuers being stopped as if tied together, the hare got safe away.
